title: Higher odd-order nonlinear Hall effect in magnetic topological insulator Mn(Bi1-xSbx)2Te4
authors: Xiubing Li, Zheng Dai, Shuai Zhang, Heng Zhang, Congcong Li, Boyuan Wei, Fengyi Guo, Chunfeng Li, Fucong Fei, Minhao Zhang, Xuefeng Wang, Huaiqiang Wang, Fengqi Song
abstract: The nonlinear Hall effect is a new member of the Hall effect family, which attracts intense research interests, and it is closely related to the quantum geometry of quantum materials. The previous studies primarily concentrate on the second-order and third-order nonlinear Hall effect. However, the experimental study of higher-order nonlinear Hall effect is scarce at present. In this work, we report the observations of the higher odd-order (third-, fifth-, seventh-order) nonlinear Hall effect in magnetic topological insulator Mn(Bi1-xSbx)2Te4 thin flakes. The higher odd-order nonlinear Hall voltage exhibits a twofold angular dependence and exists only below the Néel temperature. It reaches its maximum near the charge neutral point and decays exponentially as the order of the nonlinear Hall effect increases. Furthermore, such higher odd-order nonlinear Hall effect is observed in both odd- and even-layer samples with comparable magnitudes. Theoretical analysis indicates that the higher odd-order nonlinear Hall effect responses may arise from the Berry curvature multipoles. Our work paves the way for the study of the higher-order nonlinear transport phenomena.
summary: This paper reports the discovery of higher-order odd-order nonlinear Hall effects in the magnetic topological insulator Mn(Bi1-xSbx)2Te4. The researchers found that the 3rd, 5th, and 7th-order Hall voltages decay exponentially as the order increases and are intrinsically linked to the material's magnetic ordering. The effect is driven by Berry curvature multipoles, such as the quadrupole and octapole, and is most prominent near the charge neutrality point. This work provides a new way to probe the quantum geometry of topological materials through nonlinear transport.
main_problem: The study aims to experimentally explore and theoretically understand higher odd-order (3rd, 5th, 7th, etc.) nonlinear Hall effects in magnetic topological insulators.
main_result: The researchers observed higher odd-order nonlinear Hall voltages that decay exponentially with increasing order and are attributed to Berry curvature multipoles. The effect is tied to the magnetic state, appearing only below the Neel temperature and peaking near the charge neutrality point.
method: The study uses transport measurements on Sb-doped MnBi2Te4 thin flakes with gate-tunable carrier density, combined with theoretical calculations of Berry curvature multipoles (quadrupole, octapole, and dodecapole) using a Dirac-cone surface state Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The physical system is Sb-doped Mn(Bi1-xSbx)2Te4 (Sb-MBT) thin flakes, which are magnetic topological insulators with A-type antiferromagnetic order. The electronic structure is modeled by a Dirac-cone surface state Hamiltonian with a TRS-breaking mass term.
key_observables: Higher odd-order nonlinear Hall voltages (3rd, 5th, 7th, etc.), longitudinal resistance, Hall resistance, and Berry curvature multipoles.
important_parameters: Neel temperature (TN approx 24 K), charge neutrality point (CNP), nonlinear order (N), and angular dependence (180 degree periodicity).
main_assumptions: The crystal structure retains inversion symmetry due to random homogeneous doping, which suppresses even-order nonlinear Hall effects.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device architecture and basic transport characteristics like temperature and magnetic field dependence of resistance. Fig 2 demonstrates the direct observation of 3rd, 5th, and 7th order nonlinear Hall voltages, including scaling and angular dependence. Fig 3 details the gate and temperature dependence of the nonlinear Hall effect and the theoretical Berry curvature multipole distributions.
paper_structure: The paper begins with device fabrication and characterization of magnetic/transport properties, moves to the experimental detection and scaling analysis of higher-order nonlinear Hall voltages, and concludes with a theoretical derivation linking these effects to Berry curvature multipoles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
